steps.title: Match data to the claim
steps.content: Charles and Stephens claim that better-informed voters are more likely to vote because they are more confident evaluating candidates. The graph evidence must therefore compare high-information and low-information voters' voting probabilities, not just describe one group.

steps.title: Require a direct comparison
steps.content: Evidence that high-information voters have a higher voting probability at every political orientation directly supports the claim. It shows that the information difference is associated with voting likelihood across Democrats, independents, and Republicans rather than only in one category. That is stronger support than a single isolated point from the graph.

steps.title: Reject partial graph readings
steps.content: Choice B and Choice C describe only low-information voters, so neither supports a claim about the effect of being informed. Choice D makes an unsupported claim about how many voters identify with each party. The remaining statement directly compares information levels. The answer is A.
